coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composite and its enhanced photocatalytic oxygen evolution performance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Yumin
Hu, Dandan
Wang, Shouyue
Liu, Junlong
Shi, Ruijuan
Wang, Hongtao
coredata.dc:description: A novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composite was synthesized by a simple room-temperature precipitation method, in which the strong interaction between CQDs and Bi12O17Cl2 formed, and it was characterized by XRD, Raman spectroscopy, TEM, XPS, UV–vis DRS, N2 adsorption/desorption and electrochemical measurements. The performance of the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite for photocatalytic oxygen evolution from water splitting was studied under visible light irradiation at room temperature. The results indicated that the rate of oxygen evolution increased firstly and then decreased with the increase of CQDs content. A maximum oxygen evolution rate of 677 μmol h−1 g−1 was achieved for the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite in which 5 mL CQDs solution was dispersed. Compared with the single Bi12O17Cl2, the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites with a relatively high specific surface area and large pore volume displayed much higher charge separation efficiency under visible light. The excellent photocatalytic oxygen evolution performance of the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites is attributed to its strong visible-light absorption capability, high charge separation efficiency, and the more positive valence band position.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Bi12O17Cl2
Photocatalytic water splitting
Oxygen evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites. (a) Bi12O17Cl2, (b) CB1, (c) CB2, (d) CB3, and (e) CB4.
Raman spectra of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2(CB2) semiconductor composite.
TEM and HRTEM images of Bi12O17Cl2 (a) and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2(CB2) semiconductor composite (b, c, d).
XPS spectra of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 (CB2) semiconductor composite: (a) survey, (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1s, (d) Cl 2p, and (e) C 1s.
UV–vis absorption patterns (a) and bandgap energies (b) of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites.
Transient photocurrent responses (a) and EIS Nyquist plots (b) of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites.
Time courses of O2 evolution (a) and the O2 evolution rates (b) of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites.
The valence band XPS spectra of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composite.
A possible mechanism for photocatalytic oxygen evolution on the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites.
The specific surface area, pore volume and pore size of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites.
Performance comparison of some photocatalysts reported in literature with our work.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composite and its enhanced photocatalytic oxygen evolution performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Fuyang Normal College, Anhui Provincial Key Laboratory for Degradation and Monitoring of Pollution of the Environment, Fuyang 236037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Fuyang Normal College, Anhui Provincial Key Laboratory for Degradation and Monitoring of Pollution of the Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Fuyang Normal College; Anhui Provincial Key Laboratory for Degradation and Monitoring of Pollution of the Environment, Fuyang 236037, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composite was synthesized by a simple room-temperature precipitation method, in which the strong interaction between CQDs and Bi12O17Cl2 formed, and it was characterized by XRD, Raman spectroscopy, TEM, XPS, UV–vis DRS, N2 adsorption/desorption and electrochemical measurements. The performance of the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite for photocatalytic oxygen evolution from water splitting was studied under visible light irradiation at room temperature. The results indicated that the rate of oxygen evolution increased firstly and then decreased with the increase of CQDs content. A maximum oxygen evolution rate of 677 μmol h−1 g−1 was achieved for the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite in which 5 mL CQDs solution was dispersed. Compared with the single Bi12O17Cl2, the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites with a relatively high specific surface area and large pore volume displayed much higher charge separation efficiency under visible light. The excellent photocatalytic oxygen evolution performance of the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites is attributed to its strong visible-light absorption capability, high charge separation efficiency, and the more positive valence band position.

ce:section.ce:para: To solve the issues of energy shortages and environmental crises, photocatalytic overall water splitting for H2, O2 through solar light irradiation is one of the most promising ways to produce clean energy. Compared with H2 evolution, photocatalytic O2 evolution is a four-electron transfer process with a high reaction barrier, which means more requirements for water oxidation photocatalysts. Bismuth oxychloride with non-typical stoichiometric ratio Bi12O17Cl2, with the unique layered structure which benefits the separation of charge carriers, has attracted much research interest in the field of semiconductor photocatalysis in recent years. Bi12O17Cl2 has been widely used in degradation of organic pollutants in wastewater, CO2 reduction and NO removal, etc. [1–5]. However, the low efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole separation and the limited light absorption range under visible light limits the practical application of Bi12O17Cl2. Many researchers have found that the photocatalytic performance of Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor can be improved by constructing heterojunction materials [6], modifying with metal clusters [7], loading cocatalyst [8], doping with non-metallic ions [9], and so on. Huo et al. [10] fabricated a g-C3N4/Bi12O17Cl2 hetero-structured nanocomposite by the in situ growth of ultrathin Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets with porous g-C3N4 nanosheets and the nanosheets' structure could effectively shorten the diffusion path of charge carriers. The g-C3N4/Bi12O17Cl2 displayed an enhanced photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance compared with pure Bi12O17Cl2 and g-C3N4. Cui et al. [11] prepared Bi12O17Cl2/MXene heterojunctions by in situ growing the Bi12O17Cl2 photocatalyst onto two-dimensional (2D) Cl-terminated Ti3C2 MXene. Due to the formation of strong interfacial bonds (Bi–Cl–Ti) between Bi12O17Cl2 and Ti3C2, the photocatalytic degradation rate of Bi12O17Cl2/Ti3C2 hybrids is 9.7 times higher than that of bare Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets by facilitating the separation and transfer of photo-generated charge carriers.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a new fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial based on their various tunable surface functional groups and sp2- and sp3-hybridised carbon atoms in structures. Compared with the traditional quantum dots, CQDs not only have the most extensive source of raw materials, but also have the advantages of low toxicity, high aqueous solubility, good biocompatibility, high physical and chemical stability, photobleaching resistance, and easy surface functional modification. Furthermore, CQDs exhibit properties of photosensitization, photoluminescence, high electron reservation capability and have great potential in photocatalysis and photo-electrochemistry application [12–17]. Zhang et al. [13] reported that CQDs/LM-TiO2 composites synthesized by CQDs and layered mesoporous titanium dioxide (LM-TiO2) had low toxicity compared with the traditional toxic quantum dots. It was also revealed that CQDs not only reduced the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes and generated more active sites, but also broadened the visible light absorption range of TiO2 by the near-infrared properties of CQDs. As a result, the CQDs/LM-TiO2 composite showed an extremely high light activity for the degradation rate of methyl orange. Guo et al. [16] prepared CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2/NiAl-LDH ternary photocatalysts via a one-pot hydrothermal method, and yielded superior photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance compared with pure NiAl-LDH and Bi12O17Cl2. The introduction of CQDs facilitates the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers, and the unique up-conversion PL behavior of CQDs benefits the absorption of energy in the NIR by the photocatalyst. Zhou et al. [17] developed a CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode by co-modification of CQDs and ultrathin β-FeOOH layers on BiVO4 for photoelectrochemical water splitting. With the CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction and synergetic catalysis of CQDs and FeOOH, the photoelectrochemical performance of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 was sharply enhanced in comparison with that of BiVO4, FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4.

In this work, we synthesized a novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composite via a facile precipitation method at room-temperature in which a strong interaction between CQDs and Bi12O17Cl2 formed. The performance of the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites for photocatalytic oxygen evolution from water splitting was studied under visible light irradiation. Compared with pure Bi12O17Cl2, the introduction of CQDs facilitated the separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers, and the photocatalytic oxygen evolution activities of the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites were significantly improved.

All the reagents were purchased from Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used as received without further purification. CQDs were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis procedure, 6 g of citric acid and 2 g of urea dissolved in 30 mL of distilled water, and then poured into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 5 h and then the autoclave was cooled in the air to room temperature. The resulting mixture was collected and centrifuged to remove some sediment and to get the blue solution of CQDs.

To synthesize the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites, 1.33 g of bismuth chloride was dissolved in 15 mL of ethanol and stirred for 30 min, then 13 mL of 2 mol L−1 NaOH aqueous solution was added slowly into the bismuth chloride solution. After magnetic stirring for 4 h, an appropriate amount of CQDs was added into above solution and the mixture was stirred for another 5 h. The resulting precipitation was collected, washed, dried, and calcinated at 550 °C for 1 h in a muff furnace. The added amounts of CQDs solution in the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composite were 1, 5, 10 and 20 mL, respectively. The as-synthesized samples were labeled as CB1, CB2, CB3, and CB4, respectively. The synthesis of Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor was the same as the above process only without the addition of CQDs solution.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on a Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation within the range of 10° ≤ 2θ ≤ 70°. Raman spectra were measured in a Raman spectrometer (RENISHAW inVia). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) micrographs were observed on a JEOL JEM-2100 F. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and valence band XPS spectroscopy were collected on a photo electron spectrometer (VG ESCALAB 210). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were measured with a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrometer (BaSO4 powder as the reference). N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms were obtained on a Micromeritics 3Flex apparatus at 77 K. The electrochemical measurements were carried out using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660C) with a three-electrode system under visible light irradiation. A platinum plate and a saturated calomel electrode were chosen as the counter and the reference electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was the thin film formed by coating the semiconductor sample onto ITO glasses. The photocurrent test was performed using a two-electrode system. A 300 W xenon lamp was chosen as the light source, and 0.1 mol L−1 Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte.

The photocatalytic oxygen evolution from water splitting was carried out in a closed gas circulating system at room temperature. 20 g of semiconductor sample was suspended in 20 mL of distilled water with a magnetic stirrer in the reactor and a 300 W xenon lamp with a filter was chosen as the light source (λ > 420 nm). Prior to irradiation, high-purity argon was sequentially pumped into the reactor to remove O2 and the suspension was stirred in the dark for 30 min. During the photocatalytic process, gas phase product from the reactor was fed into a gas chromatograph (GC7890A, Agilent) for analysis at intervals of 1 h. The test was conducted for 6 h and the rate of oxygen evolution of the photocatalyst was obtained by calculating the average.

The X-ray diffraction spectra of the Bi12O17Cl2 and CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites are shown in Fig. 1. The diffraction peaks at 2θ angles of 24.26°, 26.12°, 30.23°, 32.83°, and 47.13° can be assigned to the (113), (115), (0012), (200) and (220) crystal planes of Bi12O17Cl2 with a tetragonal structure (JCPDS No. 37-0702), correspondingly [7]. It can be seen from Fig. 1 that the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites show the same diffractions which are identified for the tetragonal Bi12O17Cl2 phase. And no characterized diffraction peaks relating to CQDs phase are observed in the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites, which might be due to the low content of CQDs in the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites [7].

The Raman spectra of the Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 (CB2) semiconductor composites are presented in Fig. 2. Two bands in the low frequency at around 97 and 165 cm−1 can be assigned to the characteristic peaks of single Bi12O17Cl2 [12]. In the CB2 sample, the two characteristic peaks of Bi12O17Cl2 can be observed and there is a slight deviation of the band at 97 cm−1 to the right side. Moreover, the Raman peak at 1578 cm−1 corresponds to the G-band [18]. The Raman spectrum of the CB2 sample reveals that Bi12O17Cl2 was successfully modified with CQDs and the interaction between the CQDs and Bi12O17Cl2 existed. The intimate interfacial contacts in the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites might facilitate the migration and separation of photogenerated charge carriers and enhance the photocatalytic activities.

The microstructures and morphologies of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 (CB2) semiconductor composite were investigated by TEM and HTTEM. The TEM image of the single Bi12O17Cl2 sample is shown in Fig. 3(a), indicating that the Bi12O17Cl2 sample is an irregular agglomeration of nanosheets. As seen from Fig. 3(b), the CB2 semiconductor composite also displays an irregular 2D nanosheet structure and no changes in morphology with CQDs modification. It is observed from the HRTEM image in Fig. 3(c) that CQDs cling tightly to the surface of the Bi12O17Cl2 sheets with the diameter of 5–7 nm. Fig. 3(d) presents the interplanar spacing of approximately 0.270 nm corresponding to the (200) plane of tetragonal Bi12O17Cl2, and the interplanar spacing of approximately 0.319 nm corresponding to the (002) plane of CQDs [19,20]. This suggests that CQDs modify well the Bi12O17Cl2 in the composite and the intimate interfaces are beneficial to charge transfer between the surfaces of CQDs and Bi12O17Cl2, which is consistent with the Raman results.

The chemical state and elemental composition of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 (CB2) semiconductor composite were studied by XPS analysis and shown in Fig. 4. As seen from the full XPS spectra (Fig. 4(a)), the peaks of the C, Bi, O, and Cl elements could be accurately identified in the CB2 composite. The high-resolution XPS spectra for Bi 4f (Fig. 4(b)) show that the banding energies of Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 are 164.45 and 159.16 eV, respectively, corresponding to the characteristics of Bi3+ in the single Bi12O17Cl2 [2]. The banding energies of Bi 4f of the CB2 composite (164.40 and 159.11 eV) are slightly shifted to a lower value than those of the single Bi12O17Cl2. Fig. 4(c) shows that the O 1s peak is deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.13 and 529.90 eV corresponding to the adsorption oxygen on the surface and lattice oxygen in the Bi-O bond, respectively [21]. And the binding energies of O 1s in the CB2 composite (531.07 and 529.79 eV) are lower than those of the single Bi12O17Cl2. And in Fig. 4(d), the binding energies of Cl 2p1/2 and Cl 2p3/2 are 199.56 and 197.99 eV, respectively, for the single Bi12O17Cl2, the binding energies of Cl 2p for the CB2 composite are slightly shifted to 199.59 and 197.97 eV [9]. Furthermore, the binding energies of C 1s in Fig. 4(e) at 288.54, 286.34 and 284.80 eV are assigned to -COO, C-O, and C-C in the CB2 composite, respectively [22]. Such a difference in the binding energies of Bi 4f, O 1s and Cl 2p might have been caused by the electronic interaction between CQDs and Bi12O17Cl2 at the interface which changed the chemical environment of these elements [21].

The UV–vis absorption patterns and bandgap energies of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites are shown in Fig. 5. It can be seen from Fig. 5(a), compounded with different contents of CQDs, CB1 and CB2 samples had stronger absorption within 200–800 nm and show a redshift in the light absorption edge, as compared with the single Bi12O17Cl2. With the increase of the content of CQDs, a blueshift of the light absorption edge in absorption spectra of CB3 and CB4 samples can be observed. And the light absorption of the CB4 sample is weaker than that of the single Bi12O17Cl2. The results demonstrate that with the increase of the content of CQDs, the visible light absorption ability of the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites firstly increases and then decreases, and the CB2 sample shows the strongest light absorption ability [23]. The band gap energies of these samples are calculated using the following equation:
αhν =A(hν - E
g )1/2
where α, h, v, A, and Eg represent the light absorption coefficient, Planck constant, the light frequency, a constant, and the band gap energy, respectively [24]. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the band gap energies of Bi12O17Cl2, CB1, CB2, CB3, and CB4 are 2.18, 2.10, 1.82, 2.33, and 2.44 eV, respectively. It is notable that the band gap energy of CB2 sample is the narrowest, which means the charge carrier separation within CB2 composite is more efficient, and indicates superior photocatalytic activities than all the other samples [25].

Fig. 6 displays the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites. It can be seen that all the adsorption-desorption isotherms can be classified as Type IV isotherms with the hysteresis loops of Type H3, indicating the formation of a mesoporous structure [26]. The specific surface areas and the pore size distributions of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites are illustrated in Table 1. The specific surface areas were measured to be 42.246, 49.049, 53.480, 50.922, and 58.256 m2 g−1 for Bi12O17Cl2, CB1, CB2, CB3, and CB4, respectively. It can be observed that the specific surface area and pore volume of the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites become bigger than those of the single Bi12O17Cl2, which can provide more active sites and lead to an increase in the photocatalytic performance [27]. Although the specific surface area of CB4 composite is slightly bigger than that of the CB2 sample, its light absorption ability is much weaker than all the other samples in Fig. 5, which may limit its photocatalytic activity. Table 1 shows that the pore sizes of CB3 and CB4 are smaller than that of the single Bi12O17Cl2, and it might lead to a decrease in the photocatalytic activity [28].

Fig. 7 shows the transient photocurrent responses and EIS Nyquist plots of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites. As illustrated in Fig. 7(a), all the samples display stable and reversible photocurrent response and the transient photocurrent values of the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites are higher than those of Bi12O17Cl2. The arc radii of the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites are smaller compared with pure Bi12O17Cl2 in Fig. 7(b), which means lower interface impedance and the higher migration efficiency of charge carriers. In addition, the CB2 sample provides the highest transient photocurrent and the smallest arc radius, which indicates it can efficiently separate the photogenerated electron-hole pairs [29]. The results show that the modification of CQDs can effectively separate electronic-hole pairs and the enhanced interfacial charge transfer between CQDs and Bi12O17Cl2 is critical for enhancing the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites [30].

The photocatalytic oxygen evolution performances of Bi12O17Cl2 and the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites were evaluated under visible light irradiation at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 8(a), the yield of oxygen increases monotonously with the increased time and the oxygen yields of Bi12O17Cl2, CB1, CB2, CB3 and CB4 are 2.88, 3.35, 4.06, 2.77 and 1.99 mmol within 6 h, respectively. According to Fig. 8(b), the O2 evolution rates of Bi12O17Cl2, CB1, CB2, CB3 and CB4 are 480, 558, 677, 461 and 331 μmol h−1·g−1, respectively. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites increases firstly and then decreases with increasing content of CQDs. The CB2 composite exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity for O2 evolution up to 677 μmol h−1 g−1. The O2 evolution rate of CB2 is 1.4 times of the single Bi12O17Cl2. This could be attributed to the narrow band gap energy and a large specific surface area of CB2, which improve the efficiency of the charge carrier separation and enhance the activity of photocatalytic O2 evolution. However, the decreased O2 evolution rate of CB3 and CB4 shows that the photocatalytic oxygen evolution performances of Bi12O17Cl2 can be elevated by modifying the appropriate amount of CQDs, and excessive CQDs can reduce the carrier separation efficiency, thus inhibiting the photocatalytic activity, which is consistent with the UV–vis absorption results. The comparison of photocatalytic oxygen evolution performances of some advances in Bi12O17Cl2-based photocatalysts with our work is summarized in Table 2. The CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites in our work displayed higher values than some of the reported works, suggesting the improvement of the Bi12O17Cl2 photocatalyst by coupling CQDs with Bi12O17Cl2 to construct the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites in our work [33–37].

The valence band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were measured to confirm the band structure positions of Bi12O17Cl2 and CB2 composite, as shown in Fig. 9. The valence band (VB) positions of Bi12O17Cl2 and CB2 composite are estimated to be 2.05 and 2.13 eV. The conduction band (CB) positions of Bi12O17Cl2 and CB2 composite are − 0.13 and 0.31 eV, calculated according to the equation: ECB = EVB − Eg. The results show that the introduction of CQDs into Bi12O17Cl2 effectively adjusts the band structure of Bi12O17Cl2, and thus greatly up shifts the valence band (VB) and narrows the band gap of CB2 composite.

Based on the above discussion, a possible mechanism for photocatalytic oxygen evolution performance on the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites is proposed in Fig. 10. The electron-hole pairs are generated by excitation of electrons from VBs to CBs in the bulk phase of photocatalysts under visible light irradiation. The VB position of the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites (2.13 eV) is more negative than the standard reduction potential of·OH/H2O (2.27 eV) and·OH/OH- (2.38 eV), and the photogenerated holes can react with H2O or OH- to·OH. The CB position of the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites is more positive than the reduction potential of H+/H2, and the photogenerated electrons cannot react with H+ to produce H2 [31]. Thus, the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 semiconductor composites, with the VB position more positive than the oxidation potential of H2O/O2 (1.23 eV), is a good photocatalyst for oxygen evolution from water [32].

A novel semiconductor composite carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Bi12O17Cl2 was successfully synthesized by a simple room-temperature precipitation method and CQDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method with citric acid as the raw material in this work. The CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites displayed a much better photocatalytic performance compared with the single Bi12O17Cl2 due to the interaction between the CQDs and Bi12O17Cl2. The TEM, XPS, and photoelectrochemical analysis revealed the critical role of CQDs in Bi12O17Cl2. Applied to photocatalytic oxygen evolution from water splitting under visible light irradiation at room temperature, the photocatalytic performance of the CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites firstly increased and then decreased with increasing CQDs content. Among the synthesized CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites (CB1, CB2, CB3, and CB4), the CB2 exhibited the best photocatalytic oxygen evolution activity, and an oxygen production rate of 677 μmol h−1·g−1 was obtained. The improvement of photocatalytic performance was mainly due to its up-shifted valence band, strong light absorption, and efficient charge separation. A feasible photocatalytic oxygen evolution from water splitting mechanism was presumed. The CQDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites hold great potential for photocatalytic water splitting.

This work was supported by the Natural Science Project of Anhui Province Department of Education, China (No. KJ2018A0340, KJ2020A0543) and the Science and Technology Project of Fuyang Government Cooperated with Fuyang Normal College, Anhui, China (SXHZ202003).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic degradation of bisphenol A by oxygen-rich and highly visible-light responsive Bi12O17Cl2 nanobelts
Appl. Catal. B: Environ.

Yu, Photocatalytic degradation of bisphenol A by oxygen-rich and highly visible-light responsive Bi12O17Cl2 nanobelts, Appl. Catal. B: Environ. 200 (2017) 659–665. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2016.07.054

Int. J. Electrochem. Sci.

Mater. Res. Bull.

Int. J. Electrochem. Sci.

J. Mater. Sci. Technol.

Mater. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Bi, J. Cao, H. Lin, Y. Wang, S. Chen, BiOI/Bi12O17Cl2: A novel heterojunction composite with outstanding photocatalytic and photoelectric performances, Mater. Lett., 166 (2016) 267–270. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2015.12.089
